facts_disclosed: Considering leaving for a head chef position at a new restaurant in Old Montreal

turns.content: are you up? I need to talk through something and I can't tell anyone at work
I'm here. What's going on?

ok so someone approached me last week. a restaurateur, Sylvie Tremblay. she's opening a new place in Old Montreal. modern French, seasonal, small — maybe forty seats. and she offered me the position of head chef
That's a significant offer. Head chef at a new restaurant is a big step. How did the conversation come about?

she ate at Maison Laurent a few times, asked the server who was running the kitchen that night, apparently it was one of my nights. she tracked me down through LinkedIn of all things. had coffee with her on my day off. she's got investors, she's got a space, the build-out starts in November. opening is planned for spring
So she sought you out specifically based on your food. That says a lot. What's the concept she's building?

seasonal French, but she wants it to feel personal. she basically said I could write my own menu. whatever I want. MY food, MY vision. do you understand what that means? I've been cooking other people's menus for years. first Antoine's, now Marc-Andre's cost-optimized bullshit. this would be mine
Having full creative control over a menu is what a lot of chefs work toward for their entire career. What's holding you back?

because it's a new restaurant! no reputation, no reviews, no history. it could close in six months. Montreal eats new places alive. I've watched it happen — these beautiful little restaurants that everyone is excited about for three months and then poof, gone. and Maison Laurent is established. it's safe. I know every corner of that kitchen
That's a real risk. A new restaurant with no track record is a gamble no matter how good the concept is. Do you know anything about Sylvie's background — has she run restaurants before?

she managed a place in Griffintown for five years that did well. sold it and moved on. she's not some amateur with money, she knows the business. but knowing the business and surviving a new opening in Old Montreal with the rents they charge there — that's different. blyat. I keep making lists in my head. pros and cons. and the lists keep coming out even

When the pros and cons are evenly matched on paper, it usually means the decision comes down to something less rational — what you actually want versus what feels safe. If the restaurant had a guaranteed three-year run, would you take it?

in a heartbeat. if I knew it would survive, I'd be there tomorrow writing my menu. I already have ideas, putain — a whole section built around fermentation, another around seasonal Québécois game with Russian techniques. I could do a borscht course that would make Babushka proud and blow the critics' minds at the same time

The fact that you already have menu ideas ready to go tells me something. You're not just considering this intellectually — part of you has already started building it in your head. That's different from someone weighing a theoretical opportunity.

I know. I KNOW. but then I think about the reality. head chef at a new place means sixteen-hour days for months. hiring and training a whole team from scratch. dealing with construction delays, permits, all the startup garbage. and if it fails, I'm the chef who left a respected restaurant for a place that didn't make it. that follows you in this industry
The reputational risk is real, but so is the possibility that you stay at Maison Laurent and spend the next few years wondering what would have happened. How long did Sylvie give you to decide?

she said take my time but she needs to know by the end of November so she can start planning the kitchen if it's not me. so I have about a month. every night after service I lie in bed and go back and forth. stay, leave, stay, leave. Marc-Andre is... fine. the kitchen runs. but it's not exciting anymore. it's just work. this could be something real. or it could be a disaster. merde

A month is enough time to make a thoughtful decision, but not so much that you should overthink it into paralysis. Maybe it would help to ask Sylvie some harder questions — see the financial projections, meet the investors, understand the lease terms. The more concrete information you have, the less this becomes about gut feeling versus fear. You don't have to decide tonight.

you're right. I need more information before I spiral further. I'm going to ask to see the business plan. and maybe the space — I haven't even seen the actual location yet. I just... the idea of walking into a kitchen that's MINE. mon Dieu. but also the idea of walking away from Maison Laurent makes me feel physically sick. I'm going to be useless tomorrow. thanks for letting me talk through this, I needed to say it out loud to someone who isn't going to gossip about it at the pass

That's a smart next step — see the space, read the numbers, get the full picture. And your secret is safe here. This is the kind of decision that becomes clearer once you have real details instead of hypotheticals. Try to get some sleep. Whatever you decide, you'll make it work — you've got the talent and the stubbornness for either path.
